content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      :   File                                    Date  :  August 12, 1985
                                                          No. M-85-178


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
          Kathryn Donovan

Subject :  Advice to Diane Fellman of the Public Utilities Commission
           Concerning Disposition of Blind Trust Assets (Commissioner
           Priscilla Grew)


         On August 9, 1985, Diane Fellman of the Public Utilities
Commission requested advice on behalf of Commissioner Priscilla
Grew concerning regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18235.
Commissioner Grew has an interest in a blind trust which complies
with 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18235(b). The original assets of
the blind trust include investments in companies engaged in
activities regulated by the Public Utilities Commission.
Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18235(b)(2) requires the
trustee of the blind trust to have complete discretion to manage
the assets of the trust, including the power to dispose of and
acquire trust assets without notifying the public official.

         Ms. Fellman has asked whether, in light of 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18325(b)(2), Commissioner Grew may direct the trustee to
dispose of certain original trust assets for purposes of
eliminating conflict-of-interest situations.

         I advised Ms. Fellman that, notwithstanding 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18235(b)(2), Commissioner Grew may direct the trustee to
dispose of original assets of the blind trust in order to prevent
conflict-of-interest situations. The purpose of requiring the
trustee to have complete discretion to manage the assets of the
blind trust is to prevent the public official from knowing
whether she has a financial interest in a governmental decision.
However, since the official has knowledge of the original assets
of the blind trust, the trustee's discretion to manage those
trust assets does not prevent the official from knowing whether
she has a financial interest in a governmental decision.
Therefore, there is no logical reason to interpret 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18235(b)(2) to prohibit the official from directing
the trustee to dispose of the original assets of the blind
trust. Of course, the trustee must refrain from informing
Commissioner Grew about any replacement assets and must have
complete discretion to manage those assets in order for the trust
to continue to be a blind trust covered by 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18235.

KBD:plh

sections.question: Whether, in light of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18235(b)(2), Commissioner Grew may direct the trustee to dispose of certain original trust assets for purposes of eliminating conflict-of-interest situations.
sections.conclusion: Notwithstanding 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18235(b)(2), Commissioner Grew may direct the trustee to dispose of original assets of the blind trust in order to prevent conflict-of-interest situations. The purpose of requiring the trustee to have complete discretion to manage the assets of the blind trust is to prevent the public official from knowing whether she has a financial interest in a governmental decision. However, since the official has knowledge of the original assets of the blind trust, the trustee's discretion to manage those trust assets does not prevent the official from knowing whether she has a financial interest in a governmental decision. Therefore, there is no logical reason to interpret 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18235(b)(2) to prohibit the official from directing the trustee to dispose of the original assets of the blind trust.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a public official direct a blind trust trustee to dispose of original trust assets to eliminate conflicts of interest, given the trustee's requirement to have complete discretion over trust asset management?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that Commissioner Grew may direct the trustee to dispose of original blind trust assets to eliminate conflicts of interest, despite the trustee's discretion requirement, because the official already knows the original assets. The trustee must maintain discretion over replacement assets and refrain from informing the Commissioner about them to preserve the blind trust's conflict-avoidance purpose.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a memorandum-style advice letter with embedded question and conclusion. Text is partially corrupted at the end ('d ret ion' appears to be 'discretion'; 'order trust to be a b1 trust cover' is incomplete). Core Q/A sections are clearly identifiable and extracted verbatim despite minor OCR issues.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether, in light of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18235(b)(2), Commissioner Grew may direct the trustee to dispose of certain original trust assets for purposes of eliminating conflict-of-interest situations.

CONCLUSION: Notwithstanding 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18235(b)(2), Commissioner Grew may direct the trustee to dispose of original assets of the blind trust in order to prevent conflict-of-interest situations. The purpose of requiring the trustee to have complete discretion to manage the assets of the blind trust is to prevent the public official from knowing whether she has a financial interest in a governmental decision. However, since the official has knowledge of the original assets of the blind trust, the trustee's discretion to manage those trust assets does not prevent the official from knowing whether she has a financial interest in a governmental decision. Therefore, there is no logical reason to interpret 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18235(b)(2) to prohibit the official from directing the trustee to dispose of the original assets of the blind trust.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice to the Public Utilities Commission regarding whether Commissioner Priscilla Grew could direct her blind trust trustee to dispose of original trust assets containing regulated companies to eliminate conflicts of interest. The FPPC concluded that such direction is permissible because the official already knows the original assets, so trustee discretion over them does not serve the blind trust's purpose.
